It's better to make it an individual assignment, or if it's impossible, please make it so that we can each choose our own group members. That way, we can talk with other people in the class and find people with similar interests to work accordingly.

label: Complaint
keywords: tries too hard to be good
comment:
A lot of things - i feel as if this course tries too hard to be good that that is it's downfall
- Firstly, why are you making 20+ year-olds sit in randomised lab groups, it is so bad when you get put with someone that you have absolutely 0 chemistry with. At the bare minimum let us choose pairs and then randomise, or (the smarter option) take a page out of micr or babs labs where you literally can sit wherever. It is so unnecessary and depressing.
- The assignment was hopelessly bad - so many mistakes in the assignment in itself, no guidance or help, it didn't even gel that well with the course like you should have EITHER lab exam OR an assignment - the rest of the marks should be balanced out into like weekly quizzes or something
- Haven't even got to the worst part of the assignment: THE MARKING

The marking genuinely made me reconsider my intelligence and my place as a potential future doctor.  I knew people with really high WAMS get below like 13/20 for part A (as of this moment part B marks have not come out). The people who marked it were marking like we were going to publish the paper we wrote, it was shockingly bad. There was no consistency in marking either, someone I know got a 10, and the same one for another demo would get an 18. For the praise that I gave you guys for making one person mark the short answers for the midterm, this was the complete opposite, an absolute disgrace in marking. Any questioning of marks would just result in a lower or same mark for most people as if they took offence that they're sh*t markers in the first place. Some people's papers I read literally was like - did the marker even read their paper or did they just get a random number generator and decided any number was good. The worst part is that if we submitted that work to any other level 2 course faculty marker, they would've given good marks. Like seriously reconsider who you make mark stuff, because they must hate life the way some papers were marked.

label: Complaint
comment:
Though I partially understand why IFY courses are an established system for commerce students, they suck. 

If a commerce student wants to experiment with the different courses to determine their major, they should be able to do so of their own volition. Commerce courses are already extremely expensive and assigning mandatory courses on top of that is a poor decision. This course very much made me question my interest in being a UNSW student. There are benefits to experimentation ,however not in this manner seeing as COMM1190 single-handedly ruined my year, which was already going relatively poorly. The course was structured in such a way that made it extremely exhaustive and confusing. Having to use an entirely different learning platform was annoying at best and was made even worse by the fact that the course tutorials and lectures were at such disadvantageous times. 6-8pm? why. 

Not to mention, the course work explored in lectures and tuts was so drastically different, with the theory components almost never actually being expanded on and the focus remaining on R-Studio. Which was good in the way coding was extremely confusing, and bad in the way that the focus was on something many of us will never touch in the professional workplace. I was confused, my group was confused. My pet rock was confused. The course work was so dry, so uninspiring that simply staring at the blinking lights of my alarm clock was preferable. 

There should have been less emphasis on the R coding. There should have been more engaging lessons. There should have been no mandatory COMM1190 course in the first place. The couple dozen of hours I spent on this course made me look myself in the eye and remind myself that I've already completed thousands of dollars worth of courses and only had a couple years left until graduation. After graduation I can only hope that the job market has improved. If not, I will think back to this course and regret every choosing to complete tertiary education. I appreciate that the tutors and lecturers tried to help us, but the premise of this course overall was counterproductive to engaging students and teaching them relevant information. 

Course work should have been organised in a way that was easier to navigate and take notes on, the assessments should have been more relevant as well. Despite how much stress each assessment caused, there was little takeaway. Next time, a peer review should be included as I was ghosted by my peers more than my emotionally distant ex. Granted they did do the work (eventually), but in a way that made me regret not dropping the course. Now, this year (and term especially) has had a very bad vibe. this course worsened it marginally. Never have I spent more time on a course only to achieve negative understanding. 

Data analysis is an important skill that should definitely be taught. it felt like there was none of that in the course. or at least any that could be absorbed

label: Harassment
comment: X could improve by reconsidering her career and quitting. X's explanations were unclear and not engaging with her teaching consisting of her reading directly off every slide instead of actually teaching. Questions she directed towards students were difficult to interpret due to her poor wording and when students inevitably did not understand her question, instead of rewording her question or providing any guidance, she would become condescending and/or make fun of students. There was one instance where we were doing a tutorial activity on the differences between coupon bonds and shares and instead of assisting students with the content related to the question (which the vast majority of students were struggling with),  X quizzed students on financial terminology that was not taught. When students were unable to answer most of her questions, she became agitated and accused us of not watching the lecture or completing the learning activities before the tutorial. Additionally,  X is overly concerned with the way students phrase their answers, somehow expecting students to recite the exact solutions given by the course (this is for the qualitative sections of the course) and deeming students' answers incorrect due to small discrepancies between their solutions and the course's solutions. In qualitative sections of the course, when questions are quite open-ended and there could be multiple valid solutions,  X only accepts answers that match exactly with the recommended tutorial answers and rejects any discussions raised by students. She seems to believe that there is only one correct answer for every question which is certainly untrue for many of the qualitative tutorial questions. For example, there was a tutorial question pertaining to a pet hotel providing services to walk dogs and cats -  X claimed that the walking service for cats was unnecessary as cats cannot go on walks which really isn't true. This nonsensical black and white thinking was a common occurrence in her teaching. This seems to be due to a lack of knowledge on  X's part as questions from students often went unanswered. The one time I had a question, she laughed at my question and still didn't answer. In summary,  X is close-minded, condescending and has incredibly poor teaching skills coupled with a lack of patience, making her one of the worst tutors I have had at UNSW. She is one of the many reasons that commerce courses have such a poor reputation amongst UNSW students and exemplifies why teaching is not a respected profession. I hope I never have the misfortune of being taught by her again, however since I doubt a single student evaluation will have any impact on her job security, I wish her future students the best of luck and the foresight to move out of her tutorial before it is too late. To conclude, I hope  X seriously reconsiders her teaching methods and makes several much needed improvements so no students have to suffer like we did.

label: Complaint
keywords: forum could be used more effectively
comment:
The following feedback will be about the forum which applies to all course admins and teachers on the forum including  X. 

The  forum could be used more effectively. The answers to forum questions have been far too unhelpful or vague. I agree that if there is a direct and specific answer to the question in one of our resources such as the ICL, lectures or consultation sessions, that students should be directed towards these resources instead. However, when directing students, please give them the specific time stamp for lectures and consultation sessions or the specific topic/week/section for the ICL. Most of the responses, with very few exceptions, ask students to simply watch the recording of the consultation session or go back to the ICL. This is very discouraging as most likely the student has already used these resources and want further help/clarification or they have to sift through every detail of the resource to find a broad answer which is very inefficient and defeats the purpose of the forum.

Further, when asked to explain a specific question, please don't ask students to simply go read/watch the answers to a similar question, unless their question is specifically and accurately answered in a video/solutions. Even then, student's might need further help. During the learning process, often slight changes to a similar branch of questions can be confusing for student's and they need the extra assistance to bridge these gaps in their knowledge. The forum is a great way to get personalised responses or feedback to questions as everyone will interpret a question differently if not guided correctly.  

I understand that tutors and course admins spend a lot of time creating resources to help students and that it would be a waste to not use these. However, most students ask questions based on the content or questions we are provided. By nature, most questions will always be somehow related to the content that the course provides.  From what i've experienced, if it's a question that is related to content, students will be redirected to the course content again. This leaves the forum to only be open to questions that aren't extensively covered in or related to the course, which essentially reduces the effectiveness of forums. Redirecting the student's to an upcoming consultation is fair, but there will be cases where some student's questions don't get answered or don't get answered well. In those cases the forum should be useful. 

Therefore if a question hasn't been answered before it would benefit student's greatly if admins could answer to each student's specific questions in detail or redirecting them specifically to where their question will be accurately answered and not broadly. I kindly suggest these improvements to the forum because I have seen it implemented in other courses and I have found them to enrich my learning. 

Thank you.

label: Complaint
comment:
One of the demonstrators ( X) was disliked by most students in his lab as he was unsupportive, rude and not very helpful. It would be good to have an opportunity to provide feedback anonymously throughout the term about staff such as demonstrators so that the course coordinators can act on it.

The lack of answers for most workshops sheets on moodle makes studying for the course quite difficult as I have no way to tell whether I am on the right track or not. While it is possible to contact the course staff, it is much slower and interupts our study workflow.

The overall structure of assessments for this course felt poor. The lab reports should be replaced by a final lab report, such as done with Physical Chemistry, and more support provided. I do not find I am learning how to write the lab reports, rather penalised for not knowing how to do them. It would be good to have more in person feedback sessions and in person discussion on how to write them so that students learn how to write the lab reports, rather than being thrown in the deep end.

The carbonyl chemistry section was very confusing. While the separation of the reactions into two main "classes" was helpful, it would be nice to have some resources where students can see every reaction in each class, and make sure they know exactly what is required of them.

Accessing Equitable Learning Plans for  X was harder than it should be. Students were initially told it was unlikely additional time would be approved for the lab exams until another student pushed back against the policy. This made the course more stressful than it already was unnecessarily.

Weekly labs felt rushed and unsupported. There were not enough demonstrators in each lab, and labs were not long enough. As a result, myself and other students would be waiting for up to twenty minutes to speak to a demonstrator about something, and then not be able to finish the experiment in time. Lab techniques such as TLC needed to be reinforced more clearly rather than teaching them once at the beginning of the term. Overall the labs felt like they either need more time or a lot more staff.

Overall, the culture of the course felt very unsupportive. The course is very intense, which means students need to spend more time on the content. When students fall behind on lectures, because the workshops are often half finishing lecture content the course was behind on, we would not go to them. As a result, it felt like there was a very limited opportunity to ask feedback or get help. Some help sessions out of hours would not be a bad idea.

Lectures should be broken up and prerecorded on a greenscreen as is done in physical chemistry. This cuts down on the amount of waffle in lectures and makes them higher quality for the majority of students. Anecdotally, physical chemistry students all prefered this model to the model in organic chemistry (and I'm sure this will be reflected in myexperience).

comment:
Wayyyy way way way too much content for 8 weeks of class time. Why are there no lectures in week 10, to spread out the content? Overall though, we really need the full 14 weeks for this (ie 2 terms, not 3). I've decided to go over the content over the christmas break, if I can bring myself to open my books again, but it sucks to even have to do that.  I would expect that at UNSW, paying the amount I am, that I have more time in a term to understand the knowledge, rather than having a whole textbook thrown at me before moving on swiftly the next week to new content.
(https://education.nsw.gov.au/teaching-and-learning/curriculum/explicit-teaching/explicit-teaching-strategies/chunking-and-sequencing-learning)

Also pretty frustrating to have animal professors teaching the plant content.  X went over some things, but then  X went through it in far more detail in his lectures - kinda just felt like I was wasting time watching double. I would have preferred to the animal profs stick to the animal content, the plant profs stick to the plant content. You can see when they don't really know their stuff.

This goes for the labs as well. In particular that god damn carrot lab - way to reduce the complexity of plant science to a carrot. I love the plant content far more than the animal content, but the phd demos went over some content in a really superficial way compared to the lecture, so it was obvious they didn't really know what was taught in  X's lecture. Might be good to have lecture briefings so they don't double up on the content?

I also would have loved if the prac exam questions were made available online. I know you had review sessions - but honestly, there was no way I was going to attend that. My classes on those days were 10-1 bio lab, 2-5 geo lab. It's way too exhausting of a day to sacrifice my break to more study.

comment:
This course has a lot to improve. The main areas that can be improved are teaching quality, assignments, and the overall running of the course. The lecturer in charge although has a wealth of knowledge surrounding the subject, I felt that I actually was never 'taught', rather the lecture just read the slides and assumed that I should know the answer, rather than guiding me. The assignments, although relevant, were poorly delivered. In particular, the technical assignments. I understand being an open book with 24 hours to complete, it should be difficult. However, the content we were taught in the coursework sometimes did not correlate with answers that were to be given. Additionally, feedback provided on each assignment only covered certain portions of questions, leaving some students without answers to the toughest questions. Finally, overall course delivery was disappointing. In particular, the task notifications were filled with contradiction information about what was to be completed. For example, one of the notifications said that Excel would be needed to complete the task, but we were told that we didn't. When it came time to complete the task, the online module mentioned an Excel sheet? It was very confusing. Also, many of the notifications that were distributed were filled with grammatical and spelling errors. I understand that mistakes will happen, however when someone is paying upwards of $5000 for a course, it is incredibly difficult to justify the volume of errors thought out. 

I hope this course can be improved in future. I love accounting and I very fortunate that I have a natural aptitude for it. Im finding this course tough, but the areas I have laid out above make it even harder to complete. I can only imagine how hard it would be like for students that don't have the same natural ability.

comment:
I had been eagerly looking forward to this elective as it came highly recommended. However, I must say that I am a bit disappointed as the course did not meet my expectations for several reasons:

- The material remains at a high level and is effectively more of a 101, giving people a basic understanding of some of the underlying digital technologies rather than providing any depth.
- Having worked in the tech space for over 20 years, I had hoped there was more depth in the content and more to learn.
- Some of the concepts introduced and papers referred to were a decade old and dated. There is plenty of more current material available.
- We hardly touched on the massive boost in tech adoption due to the Covid19 pandemic and the shift toward hybrid and remote working arrangements. Even though this key aspect occurred four years ago in 2020, it didn't quite make it into the course material.
- Similar to earlier topics, the focus on the Australian market is overwhelming in the course assignments, particularly when analyzing the Australian Primary Care sector and examining CBA as specific examples. Despite AGSM's assertion of offering a global outlook, it fails to live up to this promise, creating difficulties for international attendees to engage and identify with the content.
- The feedback I received was not particularly helpful, and rather frustrating at times

comment:
Terrible lecturer - rambling. I learnt nothing from the lectures and so stopped going entirely, instead reading through the code in my own time.

Terrible lecture notes: The course lacked lecture notes, and relied on a github repository of folders containing code which was to be discussed in lectures. Presumably this was to avoid redundancy: there is only one place for all the course content with this structure. However, this structure is inadequate for at least two reasons: 1) There was no order to the scaffold. Some weeks the lecturer would talk about one folder in the github repository. Other weeks, he'd talk about three folders. There was no learning arc over the term, just a zigzag of anecdotes. 2) There was no sense for what the key takeaways were for each lecture and each piece of code. I had to figure this out for myself. Although learning all this myself was a great learning opportunity, I had to do ALL the learning organisation myself. In the end, this course felt like 10 weeks of me perusing a random github repository by myself.

The tutorials were disappointingly easy. This is a masters course - it should be harder and more theoretical. I was hoping for more hands-on coding work, implementing algorithms and data structures from scratch. Instead, quizzes, test and assignments involved filling in 5 lines of code to a code stub. For my own benefit, I ended up implementing my own algorithms from the assigned textbook in my own time. This was a productive use of my time, but it was completely self-motivated and should have been demanded as part of this course. By me delving into the details all on my own, it leaves open questions as to whether what I've taught myself was actually correct (not just correct output, but whether my style of coding was up to industry standard) and whether I'm learning the right and industry relevant content. No - these are questions that this course should have answered, but did not.

comment:
I sincerely do not understand why X  has been deemed qualified as a tutor. In the first two weeks of the tutorial, he used an almost-out-of-ink marker to write on the whiteboard, making it nearly impossible for anyone else to read. Despite classmates raising this issue, X did not acknowledge it as a problem. Consequently, no one could properly read the whiteboard, which was both absurd and disrespectful.

Additionally, X seemed unprepared for the tutorials, often spending valuable time figuring out what to teach us. He had no pre-prepared problems and resorted to searching online for materials during the sessions. His teaching style was disastrous. Even though he is a native English speaker, he struggled to explain complex problems fluently and clearly. His language was poor and often disjointed.

Communication was also a major issue. It took him about two weeks to respond to my email regarding homework questions, which is unacceptable. A turnaround time of over a week is unhelpful to any student. Furthermore, his marking criteria were inconsistent with those of other tutors. He deducted different amounts of marks for the same mistake, creating unnecessary confusion and frustration.

I strongly recommend that X allocate sufficient time before each tutorial to prepare questions that can help students understand the concepts. He needs to spend hours studying the topics he plans to cover, including homework, lectures, and questions. More importantly, he should practice and improve his teaching skills and style to avoid looking up examples online during tutorials.

His poor performance forced me to attend other tutorial sessions to get the proper content, which was not covered in X's sessions. In conclusion, unless X makes significant changes, I do not believe he is suitable for any further tutoring roles. I will advise my friends and classmates to avoid his sessions, as they have been a waste of two hours each week for the past nine weeks. I had to attend different tutorials where I received clear, organized instruction and made full use of my time.

comment: Though I enjoyed X's bubbly teaching persona, the actual content of the tutorials was not as rigorously academic as the lectures. I understand that this more simplified fun approach is better for those people who find pure academia less digestible or enjoyable, but I often found myself wishing I chose X's tutorial to get more out of the tutorials for my own learning style. Also, I found X's marking a bit puzzling at times because compared to my previous English courses where I cleanly received HDs, I only performed in the low D range in this course and I wasn't given much reasoning for it. For instance, on the first task the only negative comment I received was that Katharine didn't agree with my stance, and I am sure she had greater reason than this to give me the low mark that she did, but it wasn't explained to me and I don't think the onus should be on me to reach out to find out why. When explicit reasons for a mark aren't given, it makes the mark feel arbitrary, and it makes the tutor feel more like an enemy one has to fight against for marks than a guiding friend. These are just some small bones I had to pick with this, and I accept responsibility for the parts that are my own doing, which is sticking with this tutorial despite it not suiting my learning style, and clearly doing something wrong on my assessments, it should be noted I'm not negating either of these.

label: Constructive Feedback
comment:
There are a few things that could be improved from personal experience. 
1. Course Content and Structure
      Personally, the intention to teach multiple aspects of Performance at an introductory level did not really work well for me.  I felt like we did not go deep enough into each topic to learn about it and that there was no synthesis between each week. It was hard to draw a link between each lecture topic and it felt like we are floating between classes without cementing our knowledge.
       Moreover, there was not much "intention" in the lecture content. It felt like a mash-up of different people's voices instead of one authorial voice making sense of them. The lecture content often included quotes, videos and excerpts from a variety of sources that did not necessarily unify to emphasise a strong message. 
       It may be better if there is a better structure in each lecture where we know exactly how the topic is divided into its sub-topics and how we are going to link them back together, similar to what we've done in Week's 2 Lecture -- Breaking, slide 3. 
       In addition, it may be beneficial if there was better synthesis between each topic. This will bring better cohesion to the course and more catharsis after the whole course is taught. 

2. Balance between Practicality and Theory
      Given that the final assessment was a 3-5 minute performance, I was surprised at the lack of prac in our tutorials. We spent the majority of the time sitting down, discussing different topics and listening to insights about that week's content. 
      In order to relate the assessments better to the course itself, it might be advantageous to do more practical work in our tutorials -- allowing us to explore different forms of performance first-hand, and consequently get a better understanding of what we'd like to do for our performance.

An absolute nightmare of scheduling hell.  How does UNSW expect X to cover all this content in just 8 teaching weeks.  Very poorly set out by the school and they should be ashamed, giving less and less time to the lecturer to talk about the content and ruining the ability to delve deep enough into the subjects we cover.  So much content was left out of the lectures because of the timeline set out by UNSW, we didn’t even touch anything outside of a westernised perspective, and even the periods we did a lot was left out.  Maybe considering a change from a 2hr lecture to a 3hr lecture and take an hour from the tutorial.  The tutorials ended up being a waste as it was just us regurgitating what we had heard in the lecture and zero effort in delving further into the content.

And the assessments, what the hell.  Having us do the same task over and over again on different buildings isn’t going to get anyone to engage meaningfully with the content.  It felt like a waste of time by the end of it.  Give us an essay, a presentation, hell anything else will do.  The word limits are not conducive to critically analysing the content and keeping to an essay formatting, which is what was require.  Shame on UNSW for this, it could have been an incredible course that developed young designers and instilled a passion for history and learning about our past.  What an absolute waste.

comment:
This course is too heavy for a 6-credit subjects. The decision of having three assessments gave students only one week of feedback (on top of other subjects). I understand that the three assessments aim to broaden student's understanding of the profession, but the work is extremely heavy, especially when the last two have two separate components and only a short amount of time.
I advise the course convenor to reconsider and structure the course again for next year. As the nature of architecture is to learn by feedbacks, two assessments are probably enough to provide student a decent amount of knowledge, giving them more time to gather feedbacks and also enjoy their personal life as well.
On the final note, I speculate on the ethical aspect of archiecture school. We all talk about healthy work culture, mental wellbeing and work/ life balance. We don't want a long-hours culture because it affects our health, relationships and personal life. But what we do to the students is exactly the same as what we suffer. It all starts here.
